name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا دَاوُۥدَ مِنَّا فَضْلًا ۖ يَـٰجِبَالُ أَوِّبِى مَعَهُۥ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۖ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ ٱلْحَدِيدَ (١٠)
```

```english
Certainly We gave David our grace: ‘O mountains and birds, chime in with him!’ And We made iron soft for him, (10)
```

```arabic
أَنِ ٱعْمَلْ سَـٰبِغَـٰتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِى ٱلسَّرْدِ ۖ وَٱعْمَلُوا۟ صَـٰلِحًا ۖ إِنِّى بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ (١١)
```

```english
saying, ‘Make easy coats of mail, and keep the measure in arranging [the links], and act righteously. Indeed I watch what you do.’ (11)
```

```arabic
وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ غُدُوُّهَا شَهْرٌ وَرَوَاحُهَا شَهْرٌ ۖ وَأَسَلْنَا لَهُۥ عَيْنَ ٱلْقِطْرِ ۖ وَمِنَ ٱلْجِنِّ مَن يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِۦ ۖ وَمَن يَزِغْ مِنْهُمْ عَنْ أَمْرِنَا نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذَابِ ٱلسَّعِيرِ (١٢)
```

```english
And for Solomon [We subjected] the wind: its morning course was a month’s journey and its evening course was a month’s journey. We made a fount of [molten] copper flow for him, and [We placed at his service] some of the jinn who would work for him by the permission of his Lord, and if any of them swerved from Our command, We would make him taste the punishment of the Blaze. (12)
```

```arabic
يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُۥ مَا يَشَآءُ مِن مَّحَـٰرِيبَ وَتَمَـٰثِيلَ وَجِفَانٍ كَٱلْجَوَابِ وَقُدُورٍ رَّاسِيَـٰتٍ ۚ ٱعْمَلُوٓا۟ ءَالَ دَاوُۥدَ شُكْرًا ۚ وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنْ عِبَادِىَ ٱلشَّكُورُ (١٣)
```

```english
They built for him as many temples as he wished, and figures, basins like cisterns, and caldrons fixed [in the ground]. ‘O House of David, act thankfully, and few of My servants are grateful.’ (13)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنَا عَلَيْهِ ٱلْمَوْتَ مَا دَلَّهُمْ عَلَىٰ مَوْتِهِۦٓ إِلَّا دَآبَّةُ ٱلْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنسَأَتَهُۥ ۖ فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ ٱلْجِنُّ أَن لَّوْ كَانُوا۟ يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْغَيْبَ مَا لَبِثُوا۟ فِى ٱلْعَذَابِ ٱلْمُهِينِ (١٤)
```

```english
When We decreed death for him, nothing apprised them of his death except a worm which gnawed away at his staff. And when he fell down, [the humans] realized that had the jinn known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating torment. (14)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ كَانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِى مَسْكَنِهِمْ ءَايَةٌ ۖ جَنَّتَانِ عَن يَمِينٍ وَشِمَالٍ ۖ كُلُوا۟ مِن رِّزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ وَٱشْكُرُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۚ بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ (١٥)
```

```english
There was certainly a sign for Sheba in their habitation: two gardens, to the right and to the left. ‘Eat of the provision of your Lord and give Him thanks: a good land and an all-forgiving Lord!’ (15)
```

```arabic
فَأَعْرَضُوا۟ فَأَرْسَلْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ ٱلْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْنَـٰهُم بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَوَاتَىْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَىْءٍ مِّن سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ (١٦)
```

```english
But they disregarded [the path of Allah], so We unleashed upon them a violent flood and replaced their two gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and sparse lote trees. (16)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ جَزَيْنَـٰهُم بِمَا كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَهَلْ نُجَـٰزِىٓ إِلَّا ٱلْكَفُورَ (١٧)
```

```english
We requited them with that for their ingratitude. Do We not requite ingrates? (17)
```

```arabic
وَجَعَلْنَا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ ٱلْقُرَى ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا قُرًى ظَـٰهِرَةً وَقَدَّرْنَا فِيهَا ٱلسَّيْرَ ۖ سِيرُوا۟ فِيهَا لَيَالِىَ وَأَيَّامًا ءَامِنِينَ (١٨)
```

```english
We had placed between them and the towns which We had blessed hamlets prominent [from the main route], and We had ordained the course through them: ‘Travel through them in safety, night and day.’ (18)
```

```arabic
فَقَالُوا۟ رَبَّنَا بَـٰعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفَارِنَا وَظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَحَادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْنَـٰهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (١٩)
```

```english
But they said, ‘Our Lord! Make the stages between our journeys far apart,’ and they wronged themselves. So We turned them into folktales and caused them to disintegrate totally. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]. (19)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُۥ فَٱتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢٠)
```

```english
Certainly Iblis had his conjecture come true about them. So they followed him—all except a part of the faithful. (20)
```

```arabic
وَمَا كَانَ لَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مِّن سُلْطَـٰنٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْهَا فِى شَكٍّ ۗ وَرَبُّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ حَفِيظٌ (٢١)
```

```english
He had no authority over them, but that We may ascertain those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt about it, and your Lord is watchful over all things. (21)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses point to a portion of the accounts of David and Solomon, as Allah granted them of His bounty (الفضل). He bestowed favor upon David by subjecting the mountains and the birds to him and by softening iron for him. He subjected the wind to Solomon—its morning journey was a month’s distance and its evening journey was a month’s distance—and He subjected the jinn to work for him as he wished, making sanctuaries, statues, and other things. He commanded them both to perform righteous deeds (العمل الصالح) out of gratitude, and they were two very grateful servants.

Then the verses turn to the story of Sheba (سبإ), where He blessed them with two gardens on the right and the left so they might live a life of ease and plenty. However, they were ungrateful for the blessing and turned away from gratitude (الشكر). Consequently, He sent upon them the flood of the dam (سيل العرم) and replaced their two gardens with two inferior ones. Their land had been flourishing, but they disbelieved, so He made them mere tales and scattered them utterly. All of this was due to their ingratitude for the blessing and their turning away from thankfulness; and none are so requited except the ungrateful.

The connection of these stories to the preceding discourse on the Resurrection (البعث) is that Allah is the Disposer of the affairs of His servants, and they are immersed in the varieties of His blessings. It is the duty of the one blessed to show gratitude to the Bestower for His blessing, and it is for the Bestower to distinguish between those who are grateful for His blessing and those who disbelieve in it. Since there is no such distinction in this worldly life (النشأة), there must be another life in which the two groups are distinguished; thus, the Resurrection is inevitable.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنا داوُدَ مِنَّا فَضْلاً يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ** — "Certainly We gave David a grace from Us: ‘O mountains, echo [the praises of Allah] along with him, and [you too] O birds!’ And We softened iron for him" (34:10). Bounty (الفضل) refers to a gift (العطية). Echoing (التأويب) is the repeating of sound, derived from *al-awb* (الأوب) meaning "to return." What is meant here is the echoing of the voice in glorification (التسبيح), as evidenced by His saying regarding this in another place: **إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ** — "Indeed We disposed the mountains to celebrate [Our] glory with him at sunset and sunrise, and the birds as well, huddled together, each echoing him" (38:18-19). "The birds" (والطير) is conjoined to the grammatical position of "the mountains." From this, the error of those who claim that *al-awb* (الأوب) means "traveling"—and that the mountains used to travel with him wherever he went—becomes apparent.

His saying: **يا جِبالُ أَوِّبِي مَعَهُ وَالطَّيْرَ** is an explanation of the bounty given to David. The address directed to the mountains and birds, by which they were subjected, is placed in the position of the subjection itself, which is the gift. This is an instance of placing the cause in the position of the effect. The meaning is: We subjected the mountains to him to echo with him, as well as the birds. This is what is understood from the subjection of the mountains and birds to him, as indicated by His saying: **إِنَّا سَخَّرْنَا الْجِبالَ مَعَهُ يُسَبِّحْنَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِشْراقِ وَالطَّيْرَ مَحْشُورَةً كُلٌّ لَهُ أَوَّابٌ** (38:18-19). His saying: **وَأَلَنَّا لَهُ الْحَدِيدَ** means: We made it soft for him despite its inherent hardness.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **أَنِ اعْمَلْ سابِغاتٍ وَقَدِّرْ فِي السَّرْدِ** — "[Saying], ‘Make ample coats of mail, and measure the links...’" (34:11). Ample (السابغات) is the plural of *sabighah* (سابغة), which is a wide coat of mail. Weaving (السرد) is the crafting of the coat of mail, and "measuring" it (تقديره) means moderation in it such that its links are proportionate. That is: make wide coats of mail and make their links proportionate. The clause "Make..." (أَنِ اعْمَلْ) is a type of explanation for why the iron was softened for him.

His saying: **وَاعْمَلُوا صالِحاً إِنِّي بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ** — "...and act righteously. Indeed I watch what you do" (34:11). The meaning of the sentence in itself is clear. Because it occurs in the context of mentioning the granting of bounty and the enumeration of blessings, it conveys the command of gratitude (الشكر), as if it were said: "And We said: Be grateful for the blessings, you and your people, through righteous deeds (العمل الصالح)."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَرَواحُها شَهْرٌ** — "And for Solomon [We subjected] the wind: its morning course was a month’s journey and its evening course was a month’s journey..." (34:12). That is: We subjected the wind to Solomon; the morning journey (غدو) of that wind—which is from the start of the day until noon—was a month's distance, and the evening journey (رواح) of that wind—which is from noon until the end of the day—was a month's distance. This means it would travel in one day the distance of a two-month journey.

His saying: **وَأَسَلْنا لَهُ عَيْنَ الْقِطْرِ** — "...and We made a fount of molten copper flow for him..." (34:12). Making flow (الإسالة) is the causative form of flowing (السيلان), meaning to run. Copper (القطر) refers to brass or copper. That is: We melted the copper for him so it flowed like a running spring.

His saying: **وَمِنَ الْجِنِّ مَنْ يَعْمَلُ بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ** — "...and some of the jinn who worked for him by the permission of his Lord..." (34:12). That is: a group of jinn—as evidenced by the subsequent "they worked for him" (يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ)—worked before him by the permission of his Lord, subjected to him. **وَمَنْ يَزِغْ** — "And such of them as swerved..." (34:12), meaning deviated, **عَنْ أَمْرِنا** — "...from Our command..." (34:12) and did not obey Solomon, **نُذِقْهُ مِنْ عَذابِ السَّعِيرِ** — "...We shall make him taste the punishment of the Blaze" (34:12). The apparent context suggests this refers to the punishment of fire in this world rather than the hereafter. The wording of the verse indicates that those subjected to him were some of the jinn, not all of them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَتَماثِيلَ وَجِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ وَقُدُورٍ راسِياتٍ** — "They made for him whatever he wished of palaces and statues, basins like water troughs and fixed cooking pots..." (34:13). Sanctuaries (المحاريب) is the plural of *mihrab* (محراب), which is a place for performing prayer and worship (العبادة). Statues (التماثيل) is the plural of *timthal* (تمثال), which is a sculpted image of a thing. Basins (الجفان) is the plural of *jafnah* (جفنة), which is a large food platter. Troughs (الجوابي) is the plural of *jabiyah* (جابية), a reservoir in which water is collected (يجبى). Pots (القدور) is the plural of *qidr* (قدر), which is a vessel in which food is cooked. Fixed (الراسيات) means firm; the pots being "fixed" means they were stationary in their places, never moved due to their immense size.

His saying: **اعْمَلُوا آلَ داوُدَ شُكْراً** — "...‘O House of David, act gratefully!’" (34:13) is an address to Solomon and the rest of those with him from the House of David to act and worship Allah in gratitude to Him. His saying: **وَقَلِيلٌ مِنْ عِبادِيَ الشَّكُورُ** — "...And few of My servants are grateful" (34:13). The "very grateful" (الشكور) is one who thanks Allah time after time. This sentence is either in the position of exalting the station of the people of gratitude—by indicating that those established in this station are few and are the elite among people—or it is in the position of providing a reason, as if it were said: "They are few, so increase their number."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَلَمَّا قَضَيْنا عَلَيْهِ الْمَوْتَ ما دَلَّهُمْ عَلى مَوْتِهِ إِلَّا دَابَّةُ الْأَرْضِ تَأْكُلُ مِنْسَأَتَهُ** — "When We decreed death for him, nothing informed them of his death except a creature of the earth that ate away at his staff..." (34:14). The "creature of the earth" (دابة الأرض) refers to the termite, according to what has been narrated in reports. The staff (المنسأة) is the walking stick. His saying: **فَلَمَّا خَرَّ تَبَيَّنَتِ الْجِنُّ أَنْ لَوْ كانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ الْغَيْبَ ما لَبِثُوا فِي الْعذابِ الْمُهِينِ** — "...So when he fell down, the jinn realized that had they known the Unseen, they would not have remained in a humiliating punishment" (34:14). Falling (الخرور) is to drop to the ground.

It is inferred from the context that when he (peace be upon him) was taken, he was leaning on his staff. He remained in that state, standing and leaning on his staff for a period of time, while neither human nor jinn knew of his death. Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent a termite which began to eat his staff until, when it was consumed, the staff broke and Solomon fell to the ground. At that point, they realized he had died, and it became clear to the jinn that had they known the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — they would have known of Solomon's death which was hidden from them, and they would not have remained for this duration of time—from the moment of his passing until his fall—in the humiliating punishment (العذاب المهين) that debased them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لَقَدْ كانَ لِسَبَإٍ فِي مَسْكَنِهِمْ آيَةٌ جَنَّتانِ عَنْ يَمِينٍ وَشِمالٍ** — "There was certainly a sign for Sheba in their habitation: two gardens, to the right and to the left..." (34:15). Sheba (سبأ) were the original Arabs in Yemen; they were named—as has been said—after their forefather, Sheba b. Yashjub b. Ya’rub b. Qahtan. His saying: **عَنْ يَمِينٍ وَشِمالٍ** means to the right and left of their habitation.

His saying: **كُلُوا مِنْ رِزْقِ رَبِّكُمْ** — "...‘Eat of the provision of your Lord...’" (34:15) is a command to eat from the two gardens, which is a metaphor for their provision from them. Then [they were commanded] to be grateful to Him for His blessing and provision. His saying: **بَلْدَةٌ طَيِّبَةٌ وَرَبٌّ غَفُورٌ** — "...A good land and a forgiving Lord!’" (34:15). That is: a pleasant land suitable for residence, and a Lord who is oft-forgiving, who does not take you to task for your misdeeds.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فأَعْرَضُوا فَأَرْسَلْنا عَلَيْهِمْ سَيْلَ الْعَرِمِ وَبَدَّلْناهُمْ بِجَنَّتَيْهِمْ جَنَّتَيْنِ ذَواتَيْ أُكُلٍ خَمْطٍ وَأَثْلٍ وَشَيْءٍ مِنْ سِدْرٍ قَلِيلٍ** — "But they turned away [in ungratefulness]. So We unleashed upon them the flood of the dam, and replaced their two gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and a few lote trees" (34:16). The dam (العرم) is the embankment that holds back water; it is also said to mean heavy rain, among other interpretations. Fruit (الأكل) refers to every edible fruit. The bitter fruit (الخمط) is—as has been said—every plant that has taken on a taste of bitterness. The tamarisk (الأثل) is the *tarfa’* tree; it is said to be a tree similar to it but larger and without fruit. The lote tree (السدر) is well-known. "Tamarisk" and "a few [lote trees]" are conjoined to "fruit" (أكل), not to "bitter fruit" (خمط).

The meaning is: they—the people of Sheba—turned away from the gratitude they were commanded to show, so We requited them and sent upon them the flood of the dam. It submerged their lands and took away their two gardens, and We replaced their two gardens with two gardens possessing bitter fruit, tamarisks, and a small amount of lote trees.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ بِما كَفَرُوا وَهَلْ نُجازِي إِلَّا الْكَفُورَ** — "We requited them thus because of their ingratitude. Do We requite any but the ungrateful?" (34:17). "That" (ذلك) refers to what was mentioned of sending the flood and replacing the two gardens; its grammatical position is the second object of "We requited" (جزيناهم). The difference between *al-jaza’* (الجزاء) and *al-mujazah* (المجازاة) is—as has been said—that *al-mujazah* is only used for evil, while *al-jaza’* is more general.

The meaning is: We requited Sheba with that punishment because of their disbelief (كفر) and their turning away from gratitude—or in exchange for that—and We do not requite with evil except those who are intensely ungrateful for the blessings of Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَجَعَلْنا بَيْنَهُمْ وَبَيْنَ الْقُرَى الَّتِي بارَكْنا فِيها قُرىً ظاهِرَةً** — "We placed between them and the towns We had blessed, prominent towns..." (34:18). The pronoun in "between them" (بينهم) refers to Sheba. The discourse is intended to explain the remainder of their story that was meant to be mentioned. It is conjoined to His saying: "There was... for Sheba" (كانَ لِسَبَإٍ). The "towns We had blessed" (القرى التي باركنا فيها) refers to the towns of Syria (الشام). The towns being "prominent" (ظاهرة) means they were close together, such that one could be seen from the other.

His saying: **وَقَدَّرْنا فِيهَا السَّيْرَ** — "...and We ordained the stages of travel between them..." (34:18). That is: We made the travel within them according to a measured, proportionate, and consistent ratio; the distance between one town and the next was like the distance between that one and the one following it. His saying: **سِيرُوا فِيها لَيالِيَ وَأَيَّاماً آمِنِينَ** — "...‘Travel through them by night and day, in safety’" (34:18) assumes a command, meaning: We said, "Travel through these towns in safety if you wish by night and if you wish by day." The meaning is that We established security within them; they could travel through them whenever they wished without fear or anxiety.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ** — "But they said, ‘Our Lord! Make the distance between our journeys longer,’ and they wronged themselves..." (34:19). That is: We bestowed upon them what We bestowed of abundant fruits, proximity of dwellings, security of roads, ease of travel, and a life of plenty. But they grew bored and weary of that and said: "Our Lord! Make the distance between our journeys longer," meaning: make our journeys possess long distances in which we must ride mounts and cross deserts and wildernesses. This was transgression (بغي) and ingratitude (كفران) on their part, just as the Children of Israel requested garlic and onions in place of manna and quails.

In short, Allah perfected His blessings upon them in travel through the proximity of dwellings, security of roads, and abundance of bounty, just as He perfected His blessings upon them while they were settled. He desired from them gratitude for that, but they were ungrateful for His blessings in travel just as they were ungrateful for them while settled. So Allah was quick to grant what they had suggested; He destroyed their lands, dispersed their gathering, and scattered their unity.

Thus, His saying: **فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا** was an implicit request for the destruction of their lands. His saying: **وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ** means through acts of disobedience (المعاصي).

His saying: **فَجَعَلْناهُمْ أَحادِيثَ وَمَزَّقْناهُمْ كُلَّ مُمَزَّقٍ** — "...So We made them [the subject of] tales and scattered them utterly" (34:19). That is: We removed their persons and their traces, so nothing remained of them except tales that are told among other stories. They became names without any reality (مسمى) except in the imagination of the imaginer and the fancy of the fancier. We separated them with every possible separation, such that no two parts of their existence remained together but that We separated them. They became like scattered dust without a form, after having been a community of strength and might, until the proverb was struck regarding them: "They scattered like the hands of Sheba."

His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ** — "...There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]" (34:19). That is: in this which has been mentioned of their story, there are signs for everyone whose patience (صبر) is great for the sake of Allah and whose gratitude (شكر) is great for His innumerable blessings. One deduces from these signs that it is the duty of man to worship his Lord in gratitude for His blessings, and that behind him is a Day in which he will be resurrected and requited for his deeds.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ صَدَّقَ عَلَيْهِمْ إِبْلِيسُ ظَنَّهُ فَاتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "Iblis certainly found his expectation of them to be true: so they followed him, except a group of the faithful" (34:20). That is: Iblis realized his suspicion regarding them, or he found his suspicion of them to be true, when he said to his Lord: "I will surely lead them all astray" and "You will not find most of them grateful." His saying: **فَاتَّبَعُوهُ إِلَّا فَرِيقاً مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** is an explanation of his suspicion being proven true.

From this, it becomes clear that the plural pronoun in "against them" (عَلَيْهِمْ) here, as well as in the following verse, refers to people in general, not to Sheba specifically, even if the verse applies to them.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما كانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْها فِي شَكٍ** — "He had no authority over them, but that We might distinguish those who believe in the Hereafter from those who are in doubt about it..." (34:21). The apparent context is that they did not follow him due to any authority (سلطان) he had over them that compelled them to follow him—such that they would be excused—rather, they followed him out of their own evil choice. They choose to follow him, and thus he gains authority over them; it is not that he has authority and then they follow him. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ** — "As for My servants, you shall have no authority over them, except such of the perverse as follow you" (15:42). And He said, narrating from Iblis on the Day of Resurrection: **وَما كانَ لِي عَلَيْكُمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِلَّا أَنْ دَعَوْتُكُمْ فَاسْتَجَبْتُمْ لِي فَلا تَلُومُونِي وَلُومُوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ** — "...I had no authority over you, except that I called you and you responded to me. So do not blame me, but blame yourselves..." (14:22).

The origin of their following him is a misgiving and doubt (شك) in their hearts regarding the Hereafter, which becomes manifest through its effect, which is the following of Iblis. Thus, His permission for Iblis to have authority over them through their own choice—this degree of authority—is so that the people of doubt in the Hereafter may be distinguished from the people of faith (الإيمان) in it. This does not remove their responsibility in following him, because it stems from their own choice.

So His saying: **وَما كانَ لَهُ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ سُلْطانٍ** is a negation of all authority. His saying: **إِلَّا لِنَعْلَمَ** — meaning "so that We may distinguish" — **مَنْ يُؤْمِنُ بِالْآخِرَةِ مِمَّنْ هُوَ مِنْها فِي شَكٍ** is an exception for his authority over them through the path of their following him by their own choice. The goal has been placed in the position of that which leads to the goal; that is, the aforementioned distinction is placed in the position of the authority gained through voluntary following.

The restriction of faith and doubt to the Hereafter (الآخرة) in the verse is because the only deterrent from disobedience and the only motivator toward obedience is faith in the Hereafter, rather than faith in Allah and His Messenger if there were no Hereafter. As the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَضِلُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ بِما نَسُوا يَوْمَ الْحِسابِ** — "...Indeed those who stray from the way of Allah—there is a severe punishment for them because they forgot the Day of Reckoning" (38:26). His saying: **وَرَبُّكَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ حَفِيظٌ** — "...And your Lord is watchful over all things" (34:21). That is: He is All-Knowing with a knowledge that no object of knowledge escapes through forgetfulness, oversight, or otherwise. In this is a warning against ingratitude and disobedience, and a threat to the people of disbelief and sin.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Kamal al-Din*, with its chain of transmission to Hisham b. Salim, from Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him): In a narration in which he mentions the story of David (peace be upon him), he said: "He went out reciting the Psalms (الزبور), and whenever he recited the Psalms, no mountain, stone, or bird remained but that it responded to him."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **أَنِ اعْمَلْ سابِغاتٍ**, he said: "Coats of mail." **وَقَدِّرْ فِي السَّرْدِ**, he said: "The rivets that are in the links." Regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلِسُلَيْمانَ الرِّيحَ غُدُوُّها شَهْرٌ وَرَواحُها شَهْرٌ**, he said: "The wind used to carry the throne of Solomon, traveling with it in the morning a month's journey and in the evening a month's journey."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Dawud b. al-Husayn and from Aban b. ‘Uthman, from al-Fadl Abi al-’Abbas, who said: I said to Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him): **يَعْمَلُونَ لَهُ ما يَشاءُ مِنْ مَحارِيبَ وَتَماثِيلَ وَجِفانٍ كَالْجَوابِ**. He said: "They were not statues of men and women, but rather statues of trees and the like."

In the same source, from some of our companions in a *marfu’* report from Hisham b. al-Hakam, who said: Abu al-Hasan Musa b. Ja’far (peace be upon him) said: "O Hisham, then Allah praised the few, saying: **وَقَلِيلٌ مِنْ عِبادِيَ الشَّكُورُ**."

**I say**: This meaning has occurred in several narrations, and it corresponds to one of the two meanings mentioned previously at the end of the verse.

In *al-’Ilal*, with its chain of transmission from Abu Ja’far (peace be upon him), who said: "Solomon b. David commanded the jinn, and they fashioned for him a pavilion of glass. While he was leaning on his staff in the pavilion, watching the jinn and how they were looking at him, he happened to turn around and saw a man with him in the pavilion. He said to him: 'Who are you?' He replied: 'I am the one who accepts no bribes and fears no kings; I am the Angel of Death.' So he took his soul while he was standing, leaning on his staff in the pavilion, and the jinn were looking at him."

He said: "They remained for a year working hard for him until Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent the termite, which ate his staff (المنسأة). When he fell, the jinn realized that had they known the Unseen, they would not have remained in the humiliating punishment."

**I say**: His (peace be upon him) remaining in the state of standing, leaning on his staff for a year, is mentioned in several narrations of both the Shi’a and the Sunnis.

In *al-Majma’*, in a narration from Farwah b. Musayk, who said: I asked the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about Sheba: "Was it a man or a woman?" He said: "He was a man from the Arabs. He fathered ten; six of them went to the right (Yemen) and four went to the left (Syria). As for those who went to the right, they were al-Azd, Kindah, Madhhij, al-Ash’arun, Anmar, and Himyar." A man from the people asked: "What is Anmar?" He said: "Those from whom are Khath’am and Bajilah. As for those who went to the left, they were ‘Amilah, Judham, Lakhm, and Ghassan."

**I say**: It was also narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from several compilers of the *Jami’* and *Sunan* works from him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). What is meant by "going to the right" (التيامن) and "going to the left" (التشاؤم) is settling in Yemen and Syria (al-Sham).

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Sudayr, who said: A man asked Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **فَقالُوا رَبَّنا باعِدْ بَيْنَ أَسْفارِنا وَظَلَمُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ**. He said: "These were a people who had contiguous towns, such that they could see one another, with running rivers and manifest wealth. But they were ungrateful for the blessings of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, and they changed the well-being Allah had granted them. So Allah changed the blessing they had—and Allah does not change what is with a people until they change what is in themselves. Thus, Allah sent upon them the flood of the dam, which dispersed their towns, destroyed their homes, and took away their wealth. He replaced their gardens with two gardens bearing bitter fruit, tamarisk, and a few lote trees." Then He said: **ذلِكَ جَزَيْناهُمْ بِما كَفَرُوا وَهَلْ نُجازِي إِلَّا الْكَفُورَ**.

**I say**: It has been mentioned in several narrations that the "towns We had blessed" are the People of the House (أهل البيت) of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and the "prominent towns" are the intermediaries between them and the people, such as the carriers of their narrations and others. This belongs to the inner depth (بطن) of the Quran and is not part of the formal exegesis (التفسير) at all.
